Document [31] (Title: HIV and AIDS -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Community HIV Prevention) Primordial, primary, and secondary prevention are all important in the context of improving outcomes for HIV. The percentage of new transmissions and the number of HIV-positive people who are aware of their HIV status are key quality indicators for prevention efforts. Worldwide, 86% of the 39 million people estimated to be living with HIV knew their status in 2022. In the United States, 87% of the estimated 1.2 million people living with HIV knew their status in 2021. Primordial prevention includes addressing the determinants of health that lead to increased risks for acquiring HIV, including poverty, discrimination, stigma, or other mechanisms that marginalize groups of people. Beneficial social and policy environments are essential in primordial prevention and the prevention and care spectrum.

Document [19] (Title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 73) Direct‑acting antivirals (DAAs) encompass several pharmacologic classes used to treat viral infections, including HIV; within DAAs, cyclic amines such as rimantadin alter cellular synthetic activity, while integrase inhibitors, non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) and nucleoside/nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) constitute additional subclasses. NRTIs include abacavir (synonymous with dideoxyadenosine), dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine, and tenofovir disoproxil, and are employed both for HIV prevention (e.g., abacavir, emtricitabine, lamivudine) and therapy. Antiviral combinations specifically formulated for HIV are themselves a type of DAA. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) – including tenofovir disoproxil, emtricitabine (also referenced as emtricitabin), lamivudine (lamivudin), stavudine (stavudin), zidovudine (zidovudin), and dideoxycytidine – act as nucleoside analogues that are incorporated into viral DNA and cause premature chain termination, forming a core component of combination HIV therapy. Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) – doravirine, delavirdine (and its mesylate formulation), efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, and rilpivirine – bind to a distinct site on reverse transcriptase to inhibit viral replication. Doravirine has a specific contraindication when co‑administered with the rifamycin antibiotic rifapentine, reflecting a clinically important drug‑class interaction. Understanding the class membership, mechanisms of action, preventive potential, and contraindications of these NRTI and NNRTI agents is essential for optimizing patient care, selecting effective regimens, managing drug interactions, and reducing the spread of HIV.
Document [27] (Title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 1178) HIV infection is a viral disease that is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, a major route of transmission among individuals who inject drugs. Because of the potential for adverse effects and drug interactions, methotrexate sodium is contraindicated in patients with HIV infection, meaning it should not be used in this population. Conversely, the antiretroviral agent delavirdine mesylate has been noted to possess preventive activity against HIV infection, offering a possible strategy for reducing the risk of acquiring the virus. Understanding these relationships—transmission through shared needles, the contraindication of methotrexate sodium, and the preventive potential of delavirdine mesylate—is essential for optimizing patient care and guiding therapeutic decisions in the context of HIV.
Document [28] (Title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1662) Cabotegravir, dolutegravir, and elvitegravir are all members of the pharmacologic class identified as integrase inhibitors, anti‑infectives for systematic use. As integrase inhibitors, these agents target the HIV integrase enzyme and are employed in systemic antiretroviral therapy to prevent viral DNA integration into host cells, thereby contributing to the management of HIV infection.
Document [29] (Title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 135) HIV infection is strongly associated with shared needle use, which constitutes a major transmission route, and its prevention and treatment rely on antiretroviral agents classified by mechanism of action. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) target the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme, mimic natural nucleosides, and cause chain termination, thereby inhibiting viral DNA synthesis. Key NRTIs include tenofovir disoproxil, abacavir, didanosine (dideoxycytidine), emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, and zidovudine; each has demonstrated activity in pre‑exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) and in early or combination treatment regimens to suppress viral replication. The non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor delavirdine mesylate also possesses preventive properties by directly inhibiting reverse transcriptase. Integrase inhibitors—cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir—block the viral integrase enzyme, preventing integration of viral DNA into the host genome; cabotegravir is notable for its dual use in both treatment of established infection and PrEP, while the other agents are primarily employed for treatment but may be used preventively in high‑risk settings. Collectively, the correct classification and combined use of NRTIs, non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors, and integrase inhibitors form the backbone of modern HIV prevention strategies and therapeutic protocols, optimizing patient outcomes through viral suppression and reduced acquisition risk.
Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1227) Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors constitute a class of antiretroviral drugs that target the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme, thereby blocking viral replication. This therapeutic class includes tenofovir disoproxil, emtricitabine, lamivudine, zidovudine, and dideoxycytidine (also known as zalcitabine). By sharing a common mechanism of action, these agents are frequently combined in HIV treatment regimens to achieve synergistic viral suppression and reduce the risk of resistance development.
Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 635) Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors constitute an antiretroviral drug class that includes tenofovir disoproxil, dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, and stavudine. Each of these agents functions by inhibiting the reverse transcriptase enzyme essential for HIV replication, and their classification within the nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitor group guides clinicians in selecting appropriate combination therapies for effective viral suppression. Understanding that these specific drugs are members of this class is crucial for optimizing antiretroviral regimens and managing patient care in HIV infection.
